case.why_selected: Tests whether the schema can hold a motion-heavy, metaphor-led case where public evidence emphasizes audiovisual production, kendo, data economy, and interface guidance.

public_case_page.title: D4DATA x FLOC* - Building a powerful Brand3 for crypto investment leaders

public_case_copy_excerpts.text: Building a powerful Brand3 for crypto investment leaders
distinctive digital strategy inspired by the strength, fluidity and discipline of kendo
emergence of a decentralized data economy
The UI design delivers a refined, intuitive experience that guides users seamlessly
symbolizing the disruption of centralized power structures and the rise of a new, user-empowered data ecosystem
Datamosh techniques to create impactful transitions and MetaHuman technology to render realistic, expressive characters
capturing the intensity and precision of Kendo
We show the Path of the DATA

confidence_semantics.high: Directly stated in public copy, directly listed in public metadata, or anchored to existing local visual observations with direct visible evidence.
confidence_semantics.medium: Reasonable interpretation from public copy or from a single anchored visual observation, but not independently repeated.
confidence_semantics.low: Plausible but weak inference from aesthetics, tone, or incomplete evidence; requires human review before reuse.

extracted_facts.fact: The public case headline frames D4DATA as a Brand3 project for crypto investment leaders.
The public copy states that the digital strategy was inspired by kendo's strength, fluidity, and discipline.
The public page identifies Brand Strategy, Brand Identity, UX/UI, and Motion as service areas.
The public copy says the audiovisual production used Datamosh techniques and MetaHuman technology.
The public copy describes the UI as refined, intuitive, and seamlessly guiding users.

visual_observations.observation: The public description anchors motion in specific production techniques: Datamosh transitions and MetaHuman character rendering.
visual_observations.downstream_note: Treat as motion-language evidence, not confirmed visual behavior, until capture evidence verifies the transitions and characters.

visual_observations.observation: The interface is publicly described as refined, intuitive, and seamlessly guiding users.
visual_observations.downstream_note: This is a stated UX claim. It should not be treated as observed usability without a capture or walkthrough.

visual_observations.observation: The case explicitly ties the visual and motion premise to kendo: strength, fluidity, discipline, intensity, and precision.
visual_observations.downstream_note: The metaphor is explicit in copy; visible embodiment still requires screenshot or video evidence.

visual_observations.observation: The case positions data flow and user empowerment as system behaviors, using language around decentralized data economy and user-guided ecosystems.
visual_observations.downstream_note: This is concept/system language, not yet visible interaction evidence.

strategic_interpretations.interpretation: D4DATA appears to translate a decentralized data economy into a disciplined motion and interface metaphor rather than a purely technical dashboard frame.
strategic_interpretations.why_not_high_confidence: The interpretation combines stated copy into a Brand3 reading; no direct visual capture confirms how the metaphor appears in the interface.

strategic_interpretations.interpretation: The case frames motion as an identity-bearing layer, not just decorative animation.
strategic_interpretations.why_not_high_confidence: The production stack is stated directly, but the identity-bearing effect is inferred.

strategic_interpretations.interpretation: The interface claim suggests a tension between high-intensity audiovisual language and a refined, intuitive user path.
strategic_interpretations.why_not_high_confidence: The tension is inferred from copy contrast; actual UX complexity and motion intensity are not directly observed.

unsupported_or_low_confidence_inferences.inference: The visual execution may use glitch, rupture, or compression artifacts as a metaphor for data disruption.
unsupported_or_low_confidence_inferences.why_low_confidence: Datamosh implies a possible glitch-like visual behavior, but the public text alone does not prove how it appears.

unsupported_or_low_confidence_inferences.inference: The intended visual tone may be martial, precise, and premium rather than playful or soft.
unsupported_or_low_confidence_inferences.why_low_confidence: The copy names kendo qualities, but tone requires visual confirmation.

unsupported_or_low_confidence_inferences.inference: The realistic characters may be used to humanize an otherwise abstract data-economy proposition.
unsupported_or_low_confidence_inferences.why_low_confidence: MetaHuman usage is stated, but its narrative role is not stated.

contradictions.stated_claim: The public copy claims a refined, intuitive UI that guides users seamlessly.
contradictions.observed_signal: The same public copy emphasizes high-intensity motion language, Datamosh transitions, MetaHuman characters, and kendo intensity.
contradictions.interpretation_boundary: This is a copy-level tension, not a confirmed usability conflict.

contradictions.stated_claim: The public case claims disruption of centralized power structures and a user-empowered data ecosystem.
contradictions.observed_signal: The available first-pass evidence is primarily case-study language, not direct product behavior showing user empowerment.
contradictions.interpretation_boundary: Do not convert empowerment language into observed product behavior without capture or flow evidence.

review_questions: Do captures confirm Datamosh transitions, MetaHuman characters, and the claimed intensity/precision of kendo?
Does the interface visibly guide users, or is that only a stated UX claim?
Is the refined UI / intense motion tension productive, unresolved, or not visible?
Which D4DATA terms are reusable as Brand3 perceptual vocabulary without importing the case-specific kendo metaphor?
